natural_occurrence.description: Synthetic antioxidant with longest alkyl chain of the three gallates, providing highest fat solubility. Derived from gallic acid (natural) esterified with dodecanol (lauryl alcohol).

manufacturing.description: Produced by esterifying gallic acid with dodecanol (dodecyl/lauryl alcohol). Highest fat solubility of all gallates due to 12-carbon chain. Empirical formula: C₁₉H₃₀O₅, Molecular weight: 338.44.

other_uses.cosmetics: Historical use in cosmetics and hair products (now largely discontinued)

eu.conditions: REMOVED from EU approved additives list in 2018 due to persistent safety concerns
eu.legal_reference: Previously in Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008, now removed

usa.notes: Previously approved with limits; current use declining after EU removal

australia_nz.notes: Approved but use declining after EU removal

regulatory_history.event: SCF establishes group ADI of 0-0.2 mg/kg bw for three gallates (E310, E311, E312)
JECFA declines to establish individual ADI for dodecyl gallate despite prior group ADI
EU removes E312 (dodecyl gallate) from approved additives list along with E311 (octyl gallate)

commonly_found_in: Historical use in vegetable oils and fats
Historical use in food packaging materials
NOTE: Use significantly restricted or eliminated in most jurisdictions after EU removal

adi_context.source_note: No ADI established. JECFA chose not to establish ADI for dodecyl gallate in 1996. EU removed authorization in 2018 due to persistent safety concerns.

fun_facts.title: Longest Chain Champion
fun_facts.description: With a 12-carbon alkyl chain, dodecyl gallate has the highest fat solubility of all three gallates - it's the most 'oil-loving' member of the family.

fun_facts.title: The Great Gallate Purge
fun_facts.description: Removed from EU's approved list in 2018 alongside octyl gallate (E311), leaving only propyl gallate (E310) as the surviving member of the gallate trio.

fun_facts.title: Triple Rejection
fun_facts.description: Differs from propyl gallate by only the length of its aliphatic side chain, yet this structural difference led JECFA to refuse establishing an ADI, flagging potential safety concerns.

fun_facts.title: Lauryl Link
fun_facts.description: Also known as lauryl gallate because it's made with dodecanol (lauryl alcohol) - the same 12-carbon alcohol used in lauryl sulfate detergents.

fun_facts.title: Precautionary Principle in Action
fun_facts.description: The 2018 removal demonstrates the dynamic nature of food additive regulation - compounds used for decades can be withdrawn when accumulated safety evidence raises concerns.
